what_it_does: Defines a first-class VERIFY JURY role: a Pydantic output schema (VerifyOut), a pure deterministic majority-vote verdict rule (majority_vote), and a ROLE metadata dictionary declaring 3 varied draws, no mode_scope, and explicit jury firing.
observations: File is a role substrate (code + metadata) rather than an executable script.
Defines VerifyOut as a Pydantic BaseModel with holds (bool) and reason (str).
majority_vote is a pure function over resolved draw dicts; it counts votes and returns verdict, vote counts, n, and unanimity.
ROLE dictionary bundles id, label, prompt template, output schema, input address 'claim', model binding, draws=3, verdict_rule, rules, and render hint.
Explicitly NOT in any mode_scope; it is fired via run_jury, not part of a listening cast.
Heavily annotated with cross-references to C2.4, C1.5, C0.2/R2-FOLD H7, L2, E4, and a Tim-fork caveat.
Includes an epistemic-monoculture caveat that N draws of one model measure variance, not independent error, and a slot-in point for future provider-weighted tiebreaks.
standouts: The verdict rule is intentionally pure and deterministic over a list of draw dicts, separating model variance from verdict logic.
The file documents its own limitation (single-model correlated draws) and designs the rule signature so future multi-model tiebreaks can slot in without changing the call shape.
draws=3 combined with per-draw cache-key variation is used to avoid collapse at a memo gate.
conventions: Uses Pydantic BaseModel for structured output schemas.

Role metadata is declared as a top-level ROLE dict with conventional keys: id, label, description, prompt_template, output_schema, input_addresses, trigger, model_binding, draws, verdict_rule, rules, render_hint.
Docstrings explain design intent and cross-reference framework concepts (C2.4, C1.5, L2, E4).
Pure verdict function has no side effects and no model call.
concerns: The prompt template asks for ONLY JSON but does not show the output schema to the model; it relies on the framework to enforce schema via output_schema.
majority_vote assumes each draw dict has a 'holds' key and treats any non-True value as false, which could mask malformed draws rather than failing explicitly.
No handling of ties when n is even; with draws=3 this is currently impossible, but the rule is not robust to future even-N configurations.
The file references many external framework identifiers (C2.4, C1.5, etc.) without defining them here, so meaning depends on out-of-band documentation.
notes: A demonstrative jury-role substrate that keeps the verdict deterministic while intentionally varying the draws, and it is honest about being single-model.
questions: How is run_jury invoked and where does the 'claim' input come from?
What mechanism enforces the per-draw cache-key variation described in the comments?
Is the prompt template rendered verbatim or augmented with the schema before being sent to the model?
How does the framework select a model when default_model and default_base_url are None?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose — a first-class verify jury with N varied draws and deterministic majority verdict — matches the implementation; the caveat about correlated same-model draws is also accurately reflected.
apparent_themes: Deterministic composition of non-deterministic model draws
Separation of draw generation from verdict logic
intention_signals: The file is trying to make jury verdicts a reusable, inspectable, deterministic building block while being transparent that same-model ensembles only measure model variance, not independent error.
novelty: It treats the verdict rule as a first-class, swappable pure function with a documented slot-in point for future provider-weighted tiebreaking, rather than hiding verdict logic inside a model call.
contribution: A concrete jury role definition that a fused system could keep as a pattern: a structured output schema, a pure deterministic verdict function, explicit draw count, and metadata that records both the role's behavior and its known epistemic limits.
summary_for_embedding: roles/verify_jury.py defines a verify jury role with a Pydantic VerifyOut schema, a deterministic majority_vote verdict rule over three varied draws, and a ROLE metadata dictionary for explicit run_jury invocation.
intention_for_embedding: Provide a reusable, inspectable boolean-verification jury substrate that separates stochastic model draws from a deterministic verdict and documents its single-model limitation.
suggested_fields: jury_invocation_site — where run_jury is called for this role
draw_cache_key_format — the exact per-draw address/temperature keying scheme
provider_tag — per-draw model provider identifier for future weighted verdicts
proposed_edge_kinds: verdicts_over — relationship from verdict_rule to the draws it aggregates
drawn_by — relationship from each draw to the role/prompt that produced it
documents_limitation — relationship from role to its epistemic caveat
slot_in_extension — relationship indicating a function signature is reserved for future behavior

symbols.does: Pydantic model that constrains one jury draw to a boolean holds field and a one-line reason string.
symbols.description: VerifyOut is a Pydantic BaseModel used as the output_schema for the verify jury. It declares two fields: holds (bool) representing whether the claim holds, and reason (str) providing a concise justification. It serves as the structured response format for each of the N draws.

symbols.does: Pure deterministic function that computes a majority verdict from a list of resolved draw dicts.
symbols.description: majority_vote takes a list of draw dictionaries (each expected to contain a holds boolean), counts true and false votes, and returns a verdict dict with the boolean outcome, vote counts, total n, and a unanimity flag. It contains no model call and is designed so future multi-model tiebreaks can reuse the same call shape.
